description: The RTP Performance Dashboard is a resource to help the region understand how well it is meeting long range goals and desired outcomes identified in the Regional Transportation Plan (RTP). The Dashboard is updated each fall and early winter as various Census and other Transportation related data sources are released. Metrics on the Dashboard are focused on topic areas that were identified by the Transportation Policy Board (TPB) as high priorities for the region during development of the 2026-2050 RTP that will be adopted in May 2026.

Each Metropolitan Planning Organization (MPO) the size of PSRC is federally mandated to develop and implement a Congestion Management Process (CMP) consistent with the requirements established in 23 CFR 450.320. PSRC uses an interactive Transportation System Visualization Tool to inform the CMP as well as this interactive dashboard. More information on the CMP is available in the System Performance Report in the RTP.

1. Maintain and preserve the existing transportation system.

  2. Create a multimodal system that works for everyone, no matter how they get around.

  3. Promote a safe and healthy transportation system.

  4. Address Regional Climate Goals.

  5. Evaluate progress toward plan goals.

  6. Develop a practical financial strategy to identify revenue sources and cover costs.

This dashboard is updated in late fall / early winter each calendar year and is based on a handful of data sources.

  • National Transit Database (NTD) Performance Data
  • Washington State Department of Licensing Vehicle Registration Data
  • Washington State Department of Transportation HPMS & Safety Data
  • Washington State Traffic Safety Commission Coded Fatality Files
  • US Census Bureau ACS and PUMS 5yr data
The dashboard pages are organized by topics that an individual might be interested in. The pages can be accessed by the links along the top of the dashboard. The six different dashboard pages are:

  • Climate: Summarizes key performance metrics for transportation decarbonization, vehicle miles of travel and work from home trends.
  • Safety: Summarizes serious and fatal collisions by various demographics.
  • Growth: Summarizes population, housing and job growth in the region as well as around high-capacity transit.
  • Transit: Summarizes boardings and service hours by operator and mode as well as transit mode share to work.
  • Walk & Bike: Summarizes the number of people that chose to walk and bike for transportation purposes.
  • Travel Time: Summarizes travel time, congestion and departure time to work for residents at various geographies.

Climate change is a primary focus of VISION 2050 and the RTP, with a goal for the region to substantially reduce emissions of greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change in accordance with the goals of the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency (50% below 1990 levels by 2030 and 95% below 1990 levels by 2050) as well as to prepare for climate change impacts. VISION 2050 and the RTP focus on strategies and investments related to land use, transportation choices, pricing and decarbonization as the primary factors that influence greenhouse gas emissions from on-road transportation.

Decarbonization of the transportation fleet is an important part of the region's strategy to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. In 2025, almost 19% of all vehicle purchases (new or used vehicles) in the region were electric or plug-in electric vehicles and by the end of the year, about 47% of all new vehicle registrations were either Battery-Electric or Hybrid vehicles.

Electric vehicle registrations are all the across the Puget Sound Region but some of the highest levels of market penetration are in the Seattle, Bellevue and Redmond area. There are noticeable areas in Kitsap, Pierce and Snohomish counties that have lower ZEV registration levels and they tend to overlap with areas of lower incomes.

Vehicle miles traveled peaked in the late 1990’s at around 24 miles per person per day. In 2018, the average miles driven per person had reduced to about 21 miles per day. Over the next thirty years, the average miles driven per capita is forecasted to reduce even further to approximately 17 miles per day per person.

Vehicle miles driven vary greatly across the region. For example, even though the average resident of the region travels almost 17 miles per day in 2050, people with lower incomes travel about 15 miles per day and people living in regional growth centers travel around 7 miles per day.

Under VISION 2050, a significant amount of the growth between 2018 and 2050 is focused in regional growth centers and near high-capacity transit stations. The majority of these areas are located in the VISION 2050 regional geographies of Metropolitan Cities, Core Cities and High-Capacity Transit Communities, the most urbanized and densely developed parts of the region. As shown below, driving in these places is more like many European countries than to other parts of the United States by 2050.

In 2024, approximately 24% of people worked from home in the Central Puget Sound region, up from 6% in 2014. King County had the highest share of work from home rates at 29%, reflective of the higher share of technology workers. Pierce County has a work from home rate less than 1/2 of King County.

There are notable differences in work-from-home rates by Race & Ethnicity, reflective of the types of employment by race. In general work from home rates for Asian residents was almost double that of Black workers. In 2024, 28% of Asian workers worked from home while 15% of Hispanic workers did.
The Seattle area had similar work from home rates of other major metro areas with large tech influences such as Austin, Denver, Washington DC and the Bay Area.
Many cities east of Lake Washington had some of the highest work from home rates in 2024.

Safety was one of the key policy focus areas identified by PSRC’s Transportation Policy Board early in the development of the RTP. Safety impacts every aspect of the transportation system, covering all modes and encompassing a variety of attributes from facility design to security to personal behavior. PSRC and numerous jurisdictions around the region are working to develop plans and implement projects following FHWA's Safe System Approach, which recognizes that humans make mistakes; mitigates the impact when crashes do occur; and protects the most vulnerable users of the system.

The total number of crashes that resulted in fatalities and serious injuries in the Puget Sound region between 2010 and 2024 are shown below. After a decrease in the early part of the decade, there has been a significant increase in traffic related deaths since 2020. In 2024, traffic related deaths were down slightly but still averaged almost 1 per day in the region.

In any given year, the total number of deaths on the region's rural roadways are about 1/3 of the total deaths in the region's cities. This results in almost double the rate for deaths on rural roadways compared to those in cities and towns across the region.
Total collisions in Historically Disadvantaged Communities have been increasing in similar ways to the region as a whole.
Trends in Fatal collisions vary across the region's counties. Pierce county has the highest fatal collision rate in the Puget Sound region.
The Puget Sound region had the tenth lowest fatal accident rate per 100,000 people in 2023 among the 27 largest MPO regions.
Total fatal collisions vary by race with the rate for American Indian & Alaskan Native peoples almost 7 times the rate of non-Hispanic whites.
Younger people between the age of 18 and 30 had the highest rate of both serious and fatal collisions in 2024.
Total fatal and serious collisions vary by gender with men twice as likely to be in a fatal collision than women.

Fatal and serious injury collisions have been increasing for all modes with large increases in deaths for people walking and biking. 94 people walking died in 2024, more than double the number of people killed walking in 2010 (39)
Despite carrying less than half of the volume of daytime travel, there are more traffic related deaths and serious injuries after 6pm.
Friday and Saturday account for almost 1/3 of all traffic related deaths in any given week.

To support thriving communities, a strong economy and a healthy environment, VISION 2050 and the Regional Growth Strategy calls for 65% of the region's population growth and 75% of the region's job growth be located in regional growth centers and near existing and planned high-capacity transit.

VISION 2050 plans for 5.8 million people to live in our region by 2050, adding about 50,000 per year between 2018 and 2050. Over those same 30 years, the central Puget Sound region is also planning to add another million jobs, with a jobs forecast of 3.3 million jobs by 2050 and an annual job change of nearly 33,000 jobs per year. Observed population growth is tracking very closely to these forecasts. After dramatic job losses during the COVID-19 pandemic, by the end of 2023 the region's job base had grown beyond the high mark in 2020, but over 100,000 jobs fewer than forecast. To house future growth, the region needs to more than 800,000 new housing units, approximately 28,000 new housing units per year. The region added around 30,000 new housing units between 2023 and 2024, on track with forecasts.

In VISION 2050, the goal for growth between 2018 and 2050 is 65% of population growth and 75% of job growth to occur near high-capacity transit. In 2024, approximately 62% of population growth and 60% of new housing units were within close proximity to high-capacity transit. In 2023, about 59% of job growth was in high-capacity transit areas.

VISION 2050 and the RTP envision an integrated multimodal system that will meet the diverse mobility needs of the region's communities The RTP includes significant investment in local and regional transit; for example, by 2050 the region is anticipated to have 36 bus rapid transit routes, 10 passenger only ferry routes, and 116 miles of light rail.

Transit agencies have continued to restore service hours since the pandemic era lows and in 2025 provided over 97% of pre-pandemic service levels. Annual ridership eclipsed 190 million boardings in 2025, about 83% of the pre-pandemic ridership highs. The growth in ridership and service in the past two years has grown at rates at or above those forecasted in the latest RTP.

Regional transit boardings in 2025 continued to recover from pandemic level lows but recovery has varied by mode. Demand Response and Rail (including Link light rail and Seattle's streetcar and monorail services) both surpassed pre-pandemic levels in 2025. Commuter Rail and Vanpools, which are highly dependent on workers in major employment centers, continue to recover at slower rates than the other modes. Buses, which continue to carry more passengers than any other mode are carrying approximately 73% of the passengers they transported in 2019.

Transit recovery from the pandemic has varied across the country. At the end of 2025, transit boardings in the PSRC region were approximately 83% of pre-pandemic levels, 9th highest out of the 27 largest metro areas in the country.

The region's transit agencies have continued to restore transit service that was reduced during the pandemic and in 2025 provided approximately 97% of the total revenue-hours that were available in 2019, placing the region 10th amongst the largest metro regions. At the end of 2025, only 6 large metro regions had service at or above pre-pandemic levels.

Transit mode share in 2024 was down from pre-pandemic levels due to work from home tendencies for office workers. King County had the highest transit mode share for work trips in 2024 at around 7% of all workers. The usage of transit also varies across the region's cities and regional growth centers with the highest shares of transit usage in Bainbridge Island and Seattle.

The share of workers commuting to the office multiple times per week continues to grow and transit usage is growing alongside side it. It should be noted that the 2024 census data shared in this dashboard is still prior to the return to work mandates for many large employers across the Puget Sound region that were implemented at the beginning of 2025. When the dashboard is updated in early 2027 with the latest 2025 ACS mode share data, it will be informative to see how much more the share of transit usage grows.

People of color tend to use transit more as a commute share than white commuters. In 2024, African American workers had the highest rate of transit use in the region at approximately 9% - over twice the rate for white workers.

Coming out of the global pandemic, transit commute share for the Seattle Metro region ranked the 8th highest of all major metro areas in 2024 averaging around 5% for the region as a whole. In 2019, the Seattle Metro region also ranked as the 8th highest share of public transportation usage with more than 10% of all commute trips taken via public transportation.

The global pandemic had a major impact on transit usage across the country and places with job centers that allow for higher shares of remote work continue to see the largest reductions in transit mode shares in 2024 data. The Seattle metro region transit share is still similar to the Bay Area, Washington DC, Philadelphia and Portland. New York City continues to have the largest share of transit usage to work at 32% in 2024, down from over 40% in 2019.

Bainbridge Island, with its dependency on the Washington State Ferries for connections to major employment centers east of Puget Sound, had the highest share of workers who commute via transit in 2024 followed by the City of Seattle.

The RTP recognizes active transportation, such as walking or cycling, as an essential element of the region’s transportation system. The region’s existing sidewalks, bicycle facilities and trails provide vital connections to transit and other local and regional destinations. However, there are substantial gaps in the facility network, leaving people unable to walk and bicycle to their destinations in some areas, particularly in less urban areas.

While regional walk to work mode share has decreased slightly since 2019, Pierce County's walk to work mode share has increased slightly between 2019 and 2024. King and Kitsap counties continue to have the highest shares of people who walk to work in the PSRC region.
There are slight differences in the share of people who walk to work by Race & Ethnicity. On average, people of color tend to walk to work at a higher share than white workers.
The Seattle area had one of the highest walk to work shares of any major metro area in 2024, trailing only Boston and New York City.

The City of Bremerton, with many workers at Naval Station Bremerton living near the base, had the highest share of workers who walked to work in 2024. It should be noted that the number of people in some of the region's smallest cities and towns are small enough that the margin's of error in the underlying Census Data highlights some uncertainty in the data, a possible reason for the higher share of people who walked to work in 2024 in places like Index, Darrington and Skykomish.

In 2024, approximately 0.7% of people biked to work in the central Puget Sound region, a decrease from prior years. King County has consistently had slightly higher bike shares than the other counties in the four county region.
Due to the small sample size associated with biking to work, several data points are lacking sufficient data to highlight differences.
The Seattle area had similar bike to work levels as Denver and Washington, DC. Portland, Oregon has the highest bike to work share of large metro regions in the country.

The City of Seattle had a bike to work share around 2.5% in 2024. It should be noted that the number of people in some of the region's smallest cities and towns are small enough that the margin's of error in the underlying Census Data highlights some uncertainty in the data, a possible reason for the higher share of people who biked to work in 2024 in places like Algona, Granite Falls and Sultan.

Strategic investments in the Regional Transportation Plan, combined with an increasingly diverse set of travel options, help to minimize growth in regional congestion levels. Although the overall amount of delay for all vehicle modes is forecasted to increase between 2023 and 2050, growth in congestion per household (4.7%) is estimated to grow more slowly than overall population growth (33%).
The average travel time to work in the region has decreased slightly between 2019 and 2024.  In 2024, King and Kitsap counties had the lowest average commute time to work .

Although not large, there some differences in the share of people who commute more than an hour a day to work by Race & Ethnicity with most averaging around 10%. Asian commuters had the lowest share of hour long commutes in 2024.
The Seattle area ranked #9 for the most people with a commute of at least an hour in 2024 with slightly more than 10% of all commuters spending over an hour a day getting to and from work.

Many people who live in the smallest communities have the highest shares of residents who commute more than 1 hour. Places like Gold Bar and Wilkeson had over 1/3 of their workers commuting over an hour each day in 2024.

Departure time to work can be a useful measure in understanding if peak periods are spreading to earlier hours as congestion requires longer travel times and people leave earlier to get to work on time. Over 30% of workers in Kitsap, Pierce and Snohomish counties leave for work before 7am, 10% higher than workers in King County. The share of workers leaving before 7am decreased slightly in 2024 but is forecasted to increase as more workers return to the office.

Although not large, there some differences in the share of people who leave for work before 7am by Race & Ethnicity with most averaging around 30%. Asian commuters had the lowest share of early morning workers (19%).
The Seattle area ranked #5 for the most people with a commute that began before 7am in 2024.
Many people who live in the smallest communities have the highest shares of residents who leave for work before 7am.

The percentage of the National Highway System (NHS) that experience heavy or severe congestion has risen in the past year but is still below 2019 congestion levels. The percentage of the  region experiencing severe congestion (traveling slower than half the posted speed) is still about half of 2019 levels
AM and PM Congestion by facility is shown below for January 2026.

WSDOT, in collaboration with Metropolitan Planning Organizations, finalized its four-year Transportation Performance Management targets for highway system performance, freight and Congestion Mitigation and Air Quality (CMAQ) on December 16, 2022.

Traffic congestion is measured by the annual hours of PHED per capita on the NHS. The threshold for excessive delay is based on the travel time at 20 mph or 60% of the posted speed limit travel time, whichever is greater, and will be measured in 15-minute intervals.

The data in this dashboard is updated throughout the year as new data is released. The dashboard and data prep scripts are all written in R and the code is available via GitHub and is available for use by others interested in adapting it for their own use . The code for the dashboard is available at "https://github.com/psrc/transit-dashboard" and a set of functions for data summarization for the PSRC region is available at "https://github.com/psrc/transit-dashboard-data-process".
